author: Robert Carr
description: A double smash burger with cold 80/20 beef, a thin crisp griddle crust, shaved onion smashed into the patties, melted American cheese, toasted potato buns, dill pickles, crisp lettuce, and homemade burger sauce.
equipment: Cast-iron griddle or large cast-iron skillet
Heavy metal spatula or burger press
ingredients: 1 1/2 pounds cold 80/20 ground beef, preferably freshly ground chuck or a chuck and short rib blend
1 large yellow onion, 1 tablespoon very finely grated for sauce and the rest shaved paper-thin
1 tablespoon beef tallow or neutral oil, optional, divided
1 teaspoon Louisiana-style cayenne hot sauce, such as Frank's RedHot, Crystal, or Texas Pete
2 teaspoons Diamond Crystal kosher salt or 1 1/4 teaspoons Morton kosher salt, for seasoning the patties
instructions: Grate 1 tablespoon from the yellow onion into a small bowl for the sauce, then shave the remaining onion paper-thin with a mandoline or sharp knife.
Spread the shaved onion on a paper towel-lined plate and pat very dry. Wet onion steams the beef instead of browning with it.

Make the burger sauce by stirring together 1/2 cup mayonnaise, 2 tablespoons ketchup, 1 tablespoon yellow mustard, 1 tablespoon pickle brine, the 1 tablespoon very finely grated onion and any onion juice, 1 teaspoon Worcestershire sauce, 1 teaspoon Louisiana-style cayenne hot sauce, 1/2 teaspoon smoked paprika, and 1/4 teaspoon garlic powder.
Cover and refrigerate the sauce for at least 15 minutes while preparing the burgers so the onion, pickle brine, and spices season the mayonnaise.
Mix 2 teaspoons Diamond Crystal kosher salt, 1 teaspoon black pepper, and 1/2 teaspoon MSG if using in a small bowl. This is the patty seasoning.
Divide 1 1/2 pounds cold ground beef into 8 loose 3-ounce portions.
Gently shape each portion into a rough ball without packing the meat tightly.
Place the beef balls on a sheet pan and refrigerate for 15 minutes while preparing the buns and preheating the pan.
Spread the 2 tablespoons softened butter evenly over the cut sides of the 4 potato buns.

Preheat a cast-iron griddle or large cast-iron skillet over medium-high to high heat for 5 to 8 minutes, until a drop of water skitters and evaporates quickly or an infrared thermometer reads about 425 degrees F to 475 degrees F.
Toast the buttered buns cut-side down until golden brown, then set them aside.
After the buns are toasted, return the pan to medium-high to high heat for 1 minute so it recovers before the beef goes in.

If the cooking surface is dry before the first batch, add 1 teaspoon beef tallow or neutral oil and spread it into a thin film with the spatula. Use up to the remaining 2 teaspoons only if the pan dries out between batches.
Work with 1 or 2 beef balls at a time so the pan stays hot and the patties have room.

Place each beef ball on the hot pan, immediately cover with a parchment square, and smash very hard with a burger press or heavy metal spatula for 8 to 10 seconds, until the patty is about 1/4 inch thick with ragged edges.
Remove the parchment and season the top of each patty with one-eighth of the salt, pepper, and MSG mixture, using all of the seasoning across the 8 patties.
Scatter one-eighth of the shaved onion over the raw top of each smashed patty, about 1/4 cup loosely packed onion per patty.
Cook without moving for 90 seconds to 2 minutes, until the edges are browned and crisp.
Use a bench scraper or stiff spatula to scrape firmly under each patty so the crust stays attached to the beef.
Flip each patty onion-side down.
Immediately place 1 slice American cheese on each patty.
Cook for 45 to 75 seconds more, until the onions brown underneath, the cheese melts, and the beef is cooked through to 160 degrees F if checking with an instant-read thermometer.
Stack 2 patties together for each burger.
Spread 1 1/2 tablespoons burger sauce on each toasted bottom bun.
Add 1/2 cup shredded lettuce and 1/4 cup dill pickle chips to each bottom bun.
Place a double stack of smash patties on each prepared bottom bun.
Spread 1 tablespoon burger sauce on each toasted top bun and serve any remaining sauce on the side.
Close the burgers and serve immediately while the edges are still crisp.
notes: The big flavor moves are cold 80/20 beef, a ripping-hot pan, an immediate hard smash, dry shaved onion, American cheese, pickles, and a sauce with pickle brine and grated onion.
Do not mix salt into the ground beef before shaping. Salting the inside can make the patties tighter and more sausage-like.
Smash only once, right after the beef hits the pan. Pressing later squeezes out fat and juices.
A loosely packed 3-ounce ball gives the best ratio of crispy crust to juicy middle for a double burger.
American cheese is intentional here because it melts fast enough to glue the two thin patties together.
Potato buns are ideal because they are soft, slightly sweet, and sturdy enough for sauce and beef fat.

Use a thin Louisiana-style cayenne hot sauce, such as Frank's RedHot, Crystal, or Texas Pete. Avoid smoky chipotle hot sauce, sweet hot sauce, fermented hot sauce, or extra-hot sauces here because they can overpower the burger sauce.
If the onions look wet after slicing, pat them dry again before cooking.
If the pan starts smoking aggressively or the onions burn before the cheese melts, lower the heat slightly for the next batch.
If the patties gray before they crust, the pan is too cool or too crowded.
Use a sharp-edged metal spatula or bench scraper. A flexible silicone spatula will not scrape the crust cleanly from the pan.
For a classic burger, skip the shaved onions and cook the patties plain. The onion smash version has more diner-style onion flavor.
For USDA guidance, cook ground beef to 160 degrees F. These thin patties cook very quickly, so the main quality risk is overcooking after the crust has already formed.
Serve immediately. Smash burgers lose the magic as the lacy edges steam inside the bun.
title: Double Onion Smash Burgers
totalTime: 40 minutes plus 15 minutes beef chill time
